coredata.dc:title: Effect of different solvents on hydrogen production from hydrolysis of potassium borohydride with a new and active Ni-based catalyst synthesized by green synthesis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Şahin, Ömer
Ekinci, Selma
İzgi, Mehmet Sait
Onat, Erhan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel Ni-based catalyst supported on carbon quantum dots (CQDs), synthesized from caffeine via a green hydrothermal method was developed to enhance hydrogen generation. The effects of different solvents, including water, and ethanol, on the catalyst's performance were explored. The results demonstrate that the Ni@CQDs catalyst prepared in ethanol exhibited superior hydrogen production rates due to improved nickel dispersion and higher surface area, as confirmed by BET, XPS, and XRD analyses. Ethanol was found to effectively modify the catalyst, resulting in faster H₂ conversion compared to water. The HGR values obtained with catalysts synthesized in water and ethanol were 4662, and 12262 mL min−1g−1, respectively. The study also investigated the impact of KOH concentration, catalyst loading, KBH4 concentration, and temperature on the hydrolysis process. The Ni@CQDs catalyst synthesized in ethanol achieved optimal performance at higher KBH₄ and KOH concentrations, producing 100% H2 yield over six reusability cycles with minimal loss of catalytic efficiency. Thermodynamic analysis using the Arrhenius and Eyring-Polanyi equations indicated that the catalyst in ethanol had a lower activation energy, enhancing the hydrolysis reaction rate. The Ni@CQDs (ethanol) catalyst exhibited a significantly higher TOF of 1696 h⁻1 at 4% KBH4 concentration and a lower activation energy (Ea) of 33.78 kJ mol−1 compared to the Ni@CQDs (water) catalyst, which had a TOF of 601.5 h⁻1 at 2% KBH4 concentration and an Ea of 38.93 kJ mol−1, indicating superior catalytic efficiency with ethanol as the solvent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Potassium borohydride
Carbon quantum dots
Hydrogen
Green synthesis

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: © 2024 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs from caffeine
Hydrolysis process and repeated application of catalysts

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the Scientific Research Projects ( BAP ) Coordination Office of Siirt University with the project number of 2022-S\u0130\u00DCFEB-010.

Ababaii et al. synthesized Cr-doped Ni\u2013B catalysts supported on rice hulls and found that the activity of the Ni\u2013B catalyst in the NaBH4 hydrolysis reaction was considerably impacted by 0.8 wt percent Cr doping [39]. For catalytic H2 production from ammonia borane hydrolysis, Sun et al. synthesized size-tunable Ni particles (Ni@SBA-15) confined in ordered mesoporous silica. They discovered that the tuneable mesopore size of SBA-15 allowed for efficient size confinement and management of Ni particles [40]. Sperandio et al. prepared Ni/Pt nanoparticles decorated in a new niobium-based composite (Ni2/Pt8 NPs-Nb-CP) and used it in NaBH4 hydrolysis [41]. Mirshafiee et al. synthesized CoNi/Fe3O4@GO by impregnation-reduction method and determined that the addition of nickel nanoparticles to the Co/Fe3O4@GO structure improved the catalyst activity [42]. \u00C7etin et al. prepared tungsten(VI) oxide supported nickel/silver nanoparticles (NiAg/WO3) and determined that the catalyst provided a TOF value of 56.6 min\u22121 in the hydrolysis of ammonia-borane [43]. A Ni-MOF@nickel foam (NF) was developed by Sayed et al. to function as an anode for direct ethanol fuel cells (DEFC). In terms of ethanol oxidation activity, the generated materials performed better than normal Ni foam, and the graphene doping markedly increased this activity [44]. Ahmed et al. prepared graphene oxide-supported platin, nickel, and cobalt trimetallic nanoparticles (PtNiCo@GO) by the impregnation/reduction method. They investigated the catalytic performances of PtNiCo@GO catalysts prepared with different atomic ratios for hydrogen production and electro-oxidation of ammonia-borane. They concluded that Pt0.8Ni0.1Co0.1@GO NPs exhibited excellent activity both catalytic and electrocatalytic [45].This study was supported by the Scientific Research Projects (BAP) Coordination Office of Siirt University with the project number of 2022-S\u0130\u00DCFEB-010.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis absorption spectra (a) and fluorescence spectra (b) of CQD (caffeine) and Ni@CQDs
Adsorption/desorption isotherms of the a) Ni(0) b) Ni@CQDs (ethanol) catalysts.
XPS spectra of the a) Ni@CQDs-ethanol (before hydrolysis), b) Ni@CQDs-water (before hydrolysis), c) Ni@CQDs-ethanol (after hydrolysis), d) Ni@CQDs-water (after hydrolysis).
XRD spectra of a) Ni@CQDs (ethanol) before use b) Ni@CQDs (ethanol) after use c) Ni@CQDs (water) before use d) Ni@CQDs (water) after use e) Ni (0).
TEM images of a) CQDs, b) Ni@Caffein, c)Ni@CQDs (water), d)Ni@CQDs (ethanol).
FT-IR analysis of a) Ni(0), Ni@Caffein, Ni@CQDs (water), Ni@CQDs (ethanol), b) Ni@CQDs ethanol (before and after use), c) Ni@CQDs water (before and after use).
The effect of metal loading method in water solvent.
The effect of different solvents on the catalytic activity.
The effect of KOH concentration on hydrolysis with a) Ni@CQDs (water) b) Ni@CQDs (ethanol).
The effect of catalyst amount on hydrolysis with a) Ni@CQDs (water) b) Ni@CQDs (ethanol).
The effect of KBH4 concentration on hydrolysis with a) Ni@CQDs (water) b) Ni@CQDs (ethanol).
The effect of tempereture on hydrolysis with a) Ni@CQDs (water) b) Ni@CQDs (ethanol).
The Arrhenius plots of the hydrolysis of KBH4 with a) Ni@CQDs (water) b) Ni@CQDs (ethanol).
The reusabilty of catalyst a) Ni@CQDs (water) b) Ni@CQDs (ethanol).

head.ce:title: Effect of different solvents on hydrogen production from hydrolysis of potassium borohydride with a new and active Ni-based catalyst synthesized by green synthesis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Vocational School of Organised Industrial Zone, Department of Electrical and Energy, Bitlis Eren University, Bitlis, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Vocational School of Organised Industrial Zone

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Vocational School of Organised Industrial Zone, Department of Electrical and Energy, Bitlis Eren University, Bitlis, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel Ni-based catalyst supported on carbon quantum dots (CQDs), synthesized from caffeine via a green hydrothermal method was developed to enhance hydrogen generation. The effects of different solvents, including water, and ethanol, on the catalyst's performance were explored. The results demonstrate that the Ni@CQDs catalyst prepared in ethanol exhibited superior hydrogen production rates due to improved nickel dispersion and higher surface area, as confirmed by BET, XPS, and XRD analyses. Ethanol was found to effectively modify the catalyst, resulting in faster H₂ conversion compared to water. The HGR values obtained with catalysts synthesized in water and ethanol were 4662, and 12262 mL min−1g−1, respectively. The study also investigated the impact of KOH concentration, catalyst loading, KBH4 concentration, and temperature on the hydrolysis process. The Ni@CQDs catalyst synthesized in ethanol achieved optimal performance at higher KBH₄ and KOH concentrations, producing 100% H2 yield over six reusability cycles with minimal loss of catalytic efficiency. Thermodynamic analysis using the Arrhenius and Eyring-Polanyi equations indicated that the catalyst in ethanol had a lower activation energy, enhancing the hydrolysis reaction rate. The Ni@CQDs (ethanol) catalyst exhibited a significantly higher TOF of 1696 h⁻1 at 4% KBH4 concentration and a lower activation energy (Ea) of 33.78 kJ mol−1 compared to the Ni@CQDs (water) catalyst, which had a TOF of 601.5 h⁻1 at 2% KBH4 concentration and an Ea of 38.93 kJ mol−1, indicating superior catalytic efficiency with ethanol as the solvent.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen has great potential as a clean and renewable energy source. Its use in energy systems is seen as important, especially as a replacement for fossil fuels. Hydrogen is environmentally beneficial since it only emits water vapour and without any other hazardous emissions when it burns or is utilized in fuel cells [1–6]. It is regarded as a key solution, especially for achieving carbon neutral energy targets. However, there are some obstacles that prevent hydrogen from widely replacing fossil fuels. The most significant of these obstacles are the difficulties and costs associated with the production, storage and transportation of hydrogen [7–9]. The challenges of hydrogen transport and storage can be addressed by chemically storing it in boron-based compounds. In solid form, hydrogen becomes easier to store and transport [10–14]. Due to this advantage, the interaction between hydrogen and boron has become a highly researched topic. Technological advancements and energy transition policies in this area will allow hydrogen to play a significant role in global energy systems [15,16].

Boron hydride compounds are among the most interesting hydrogen storage and transportation materials. When boron hydrides react with water, large amounts of hydrogen gas can be produced. This reaction often occurs at room temperature with simple catalysts, making it an energy-efficient technique [17,18]. Due to their high hydrogen density, boron hydrides are desirable for hydrogen carriers in energy and transportation applications. After hydrolysis, the byproducts can be recycled, creating a sustainable cycle. However, before this technology can be widely adopted, more research is needed on system integration and cost-effectiveness [19,20]. Compounds like sodium boron hydride (KBH4), lithium boron hydride (LiBH4), and ammonia borane (NH3BH3) are used to produce and store hydrogen [21,22].

Potassium borohydride (PBH) can be used as a solid in various fuel cells due to its 7.2% mass hydrogen storage capacity and ability to function in solid form at temperatures as high as 400 °C. Since it is solid, it is safer to transport and store than gaseous hydrogen or other liquid fuels. PBH is stable under appropriate conditions and does not release hydrogen unless it reacts with water, which provides advantages for long-term storage [23,24]. The reaction of PBH with water during hydrolysis to produce hydrogen gas is shown by the following general equation (1):
(1)
K B
H 4
+ 2
H 2
O → K B
O 2
+ 4
H
2

Theoretically, 4 mol of hydrogen gas can be produced from the reaction. This means that PBH is an effective source of hydrogen [25]. The hydrolysis reaction can usually be accelerated with the aid of a catalyst. Catalysts accelerate the hydrolysis reaction, which progresses slowly at room temperature or under normal conditions, by lowering the activation energy. This is important for practical use because hydrogen must be produced and used as soon as it is demanded [26]. By reducing the possibility of side reactions, catalysts maximize the hydrogen generation. Furthermore, they typically permit reactions to occur at lower temperatures, eliminating the need for external heating and lowering energy expenses [27].

Numerous investigations have been conducted on heterogeneous or homogeneous catalyst systems with metallic nanoparticles for the formation of H2 through hydrolysis reactions. Metal catalyst systems like nickel (Ni) and cobalt (Co) have also been employed; however, because of their inert catalytic structures, significant morphological and interface changes are necessary to increase the effectiveness of these catalysts [28–37]. Moreover, there are still issues with poor dispersion and easy aggregation with pure metals and their compounds. Therefore, support materials are increasingly used to minimize the amount of precious metal required, thus lowering costs, while also maximizing active surface area to enhance the durability and effectiveness of metal catalysts [38]. While many studies have been conducted on the hydrolysis of sodium borohydride using Ni-based catalysts, few have focused on hydrogen production through the hydrolysis of PBH.

Ababaii et al. synthesized Cr-doped Ni–B catalysts supported on rice hulls and found that the activity of the Ni–B catalyst in the NaBH4 hydrolysis reaction was considerably impacted by 0.8 wt percent Cr doping [39]. For catalytic H2 production from ammonia borane hydrolysis, Sun et al. synthesized size-tunable Ni particles (Ni@SBA-15) confined in ordered mesoporous silica. They discovered that the tuneable mesopore size of SBA-15 allowed for efficient size confinement and management of Ni particles [40]. Sperandio et al. prepared Ni/Pt nanoparticles decorated in a new niobium-based composite (Ni2/Pt8 NPs-Nb-CP) and used it in NaBH4 hydrolysis [41]. Mirshafiee et al. synthesized CoNi/Fe3O4@GO by impregnation-reduction method and determined that the addition of nickel nanoparticles to the Co/Fe3O4@GO structure improved the catalyst activity [42]. Çetin et al. prepared tungsten(VI) oxide supported nickel/silver nanoparticles (NiAg/WO3) and determined that the catalyst provided a TOF value of 56.6 min−1 in the hydrolysis of ammonia-borane [43]. A Ni-MOF@nickel foam (NF) was developed by Sayed et al. to function as an anode for direct ethanol fuel cells (DEFC). In terms of ethanol oxidation activity, the generated materials performed better than normal Ni foam, and the graphene doping markedly increased this activity [44]. Ahmed et al. prepared graphene oxide-supported platin, nickel, and cobalt trimetallic nanoparticles (PtNiCo@GO) by the impregnation/reduction method. They investigated the catalytic performances of PtNiCo@GO catalysts prepared with different atomic ratios for hydrogen production and electro-oxidation of ammonia-borane. They concluded that Pt0.8Ni0.1Co0.1@GO NPs exhibited excellent activity both catalytic and electrocatalytic [45].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are quantum particles at the nanometer scale (typically 2–10 nm) composed of carbon atoms. Their electronic and optical properties are size-dependent, making them highly attractive, particularly due to their environmental friendliness, biocompatibility, and ease of production [46–51]. The surface of CQDs can be coated or modified with various functional groups (e.g. carboxyl (-COOH), hydroxyl (-OH), amine (-NH₂)), enhancing their water solubility and enabling tailored interactions with biological and chemical systems. Due to their fluorescent properties, biocompatibility, chemical stability, and environmental friendliness, CQDs have significant applications in biomedical, environmental, energy storage, and optoelectronic fields. Catalytic performance can be enhanced by using inexpensive, easily maintained, and ultra-small carbon quantum dots as support materials in catalyst applications via the hydrothermal process [52–55].

In this study, CQDs synthesized hydrothermally from caffeine were used as a support material for Ni metal in the hydrolysis of KBH4 to produce hydrogen. The research was conducted in three stages: (i) synthesis of carbon quantum dots, (ii) catalyst synthesis by metal loading on CQDs and its characterization, and (iii) application of the synthesized catalysts in the hydrolysis of PBH, along with the investigation of catalytic parameters.

The following materials were used in this research: KOH from Sigma-Aldrich, KBH4 from Acros, caffeine and NiCI2.6H2O from Sigma-Aldrich, and methanol and ethanol from Tekkim. The oven (Nova 300), the magnetic stirrer (Heidolph), the electronic balance (Mettler Toledo), the autoclave and vacuum pump (ilmvac) were the instruments used in the study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs from caffeine
ce:section.ce:para: An appropriate amount of caffeine and pure water were placed in an autoclave and heated in an oven. The baking time and temperature varied depending on the dispersion of the organic compound. Therefore, different temperatures and durations were tested. The optimal synthesis conditions were determined based on the photoluminescence effect under UV light (360 nm). Optimum conditions were achieved using 0.01 mol of caffeine. After metal loading, the transformation of caffeine into CQDs can be inferred based on the amount of heterogeneous catalyst, as no fluorescence effect was observed under UV light after the reduction process. Accordingly, it was determined that 46% of the caffeine used initially was incorporated into the catalyst structure. The resulting CQDs were filtered through a 0.45-μm filter using pressure filtration to remove impurities. The filtered CQDs were then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 60 min, and sedimentation was examined. However, no sedimentation was observed after centrifugation. Under ideal conditions, a sufficient quantity of CQDs was produced and used for catalyst synthesis [56].

A supported catalyst was prepared using 6–15 mg of Ni metal with a fixed amount of CQDs. Two distinct techniques—the impregnation method and the hydrothermal approach—were applied. Ni reduction was carried out using 10 mmol of NaBH4. After 48–60 h, Ni species initiated the precipitation process. Centrifugation for 45 min ensured complete precipitation of the nickel species. Additionally, the CQDs-supported Ni catalyst was prepared using different solvents—ethanol and water. The catalysts prepared via the impregnation method were named Ni@caffeine and Ni@DOT, while the hydrothermal method produced a catalyst named Ni@MOF-DOT. A pure, unloaded Ni(0) catalyst was also used for comparison.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrolysis process and repeated application of catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental setup for producing hydrogen via KBH₄ hydrolysis consisted of a reactor for the reaction, a water bath to maintain the desired temperature, a gas burette to collect the produced hydrogen, and gas hoses to transport the hydrogen from the reactor to the collection unit. Before starting the experiment, a specified amount of catalyst and KOH were placed in the reaction vessel. KBH₄ was then added and heated to a specific temperature in the water bath. The volume of H₂ generated after the reaction began was measured using an inverted burette and the traditional water displacement method. The effects of KBH₄ and KOH concentrations, temperature, catalyst amount, hydrothermal duration, and the Ni metal/CQDs ratio were investigated in the hydrolysis of KBH₄.

The average hydrogen generation rate (HGR) parameter was used to evaluate the catalyst's efficiency. The catalyst underwent seven consecutive trials to assess its repeatability. After each experiment, 90% of the liquid fraction was decanted, and fresh KBH4was added.

The HGR is calculated using the following formula (Equ. 2):
(2)
r =
v
(
m L
)
t
( min )
m
c a t
( g )

The parameters used in the equation are: V is the total volume of hydrogen; r is the rate; m
cat is the catalyst quantity; and t is the time.

A combination of techniques was employed to comprehensively analyze the structural, chemical, and physical properties of the Ni nanocatalyst. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI 5000 VersaProbe) was used to examine the surface chemistry, oxidation states of elements, and their binding energies. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM 1220), ideal for high-resolution analysis, was applied to study the internal structure, particle sizes, and arrangement of the nanocatalyst. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX, HITACHI TM 3030 Plus) provided information on the elemental distribution, while X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Ultima-IV) patterns revealed the crystal dimensions and structural regularity of the material. Additionally, BET (Quantachrome Corporation, Autosorb-6) and FT-IR (Shimadzu IRTracer™-100) analyses were also carried out to determine the characteristics of the catalyst surface and the presence of functional groups on it.

Fig. 1 displays the UV–Vis (a) and fluorescence absorption spectra (b) of CQD and Ni@CQD. According to the UV–Vis spectra, CQD and Ni@CQD exhibit strong absorption in the UV range (250–300 nm), with the highest absorption peak occurring at 278 nm as a result of the π-π∗ conjugate transition of CC and the n-π∗ transition of C–O. In comparison to CQD, Ni@CQD exhibits a weaker absorption peak intensity [57]. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra for CQD and Ni@CQD samples at 360 nm are shown in Fig. 1(b). The emission peak for CQD is located at 360 nm. The addition of nickel atoms to CQD considerably lowers the PL density. This validates the synthesized Ni@CQD sample's enhanced quantum excess. This finding suggests that Ni atoms have bonded to the CQD surface and that their electron transitions on the surface have narrowed [3].

The XPS spectra of the Ni@CQDs (water) and Ni@CQDs (ethanol) catalysts are shown in Fig. 3 . Both catalysts exhibit similar chemical properties, as indicated by the spectra. Peaks corresponding to the nickel 2p3/2 orbitals are present in both samples, suggesting that nickel exists in an oxidized form (e.g. NiO). There is no significant shift in the chemical state of nickel for either catalyst, as the peak positions remain nearly identical. The C 1s and O 1s peaks are more prominent in the XPS spectrum of the Ni@CQDs catalyst synthesized in ethanol. Furthermore, the peak at 856 eV (Ni 2p3/2) is sharper and more pronounced in the ethanol-produced catalyst compared to the one synthesized in water. This suggests that CQDs are more uniformly dispersed on nickel in the ethanol medium, potentially leading to enhanced catalytic activity [58].

The XRD patterns of the samples are presented in Fig. 4 . All catalysts show a broad Ni peak at 2θ = 45°. While both samples exhibit similar Ni and Ni(OH)2 peaks, careful inspection reveals that the Ni peak in Ni@CQDs (ethanol) is slightly sharper, whereas the Ni(OH)2 peak in Ni@CQDs (water) is more pronounced. These subtle differences could impact catalytic performance, reflecting changes in phase composition and distribution influenced by the solvent. A sharper Ni peak suggests higher crystalline purity and less oxidation, indicating a more active nickel phase. Conversely, the broader Ni peak and stronger Ni(OH)2 presence in Ni@CQDs (water) suggest greater oxidation and structural disorder, potentially reducing catalytic activity [59].

TEM images of the Ni catalyst samples are shown in Fig. 5 . The average particle sizes of CQDs, Ni@Caffeine, Ni@CQDs (water), and Ni@CQDs (ethanol) were measured as 3.15, 11.6, 3.42, and 2.54 nm, respectively. The images reveal that CQDs exhibit varying morphological properties when dispersed on the nickel catalyst in different environments. Additionally, the significant decrease in particle size of Ni@CQDs (water) and Ni@CQDs (ethanol) compared to Ni@Caffeine can be attributed to the superior dispersion and stabilization properties of CQDs. Aggregation is prevented by the weakening of van der Waals bonds between CQDs subjected to hydrothermal treatment. This controls the growth of Ni nanoclusters and provides them with a large number of functional groups and binding sites. In addition, the polar nature of water and ethanol used in the synthesis increases the interaction between Ni precursors and CQDs, while the graphitic structure of CQDs strengthens metal-support interactions, leading to smaller, more uniform particles [4].

FT-IR analysis was conducted to identify the functional groups present in the catalyst samples (Fig. 6 ). Similar functional groups were observed across all spectra. The Ni@CQDs (ethanol) and Ni@CQDs (water) samples showed higher peak intensities compared to Ni@Caffeine, due to the greater presence of oxygen-containing active groups. The peaks between 1500 and 1600 cm−1 are significant as they are associated with Lewis and Brønsted acid sites, indicating substantial Lewis acid sites in both the Ni@CQDs (ethanol) and Ni@CQDs (water) catalysts. The FT-IR bands below 2000 cm−1 typically correspond to CO stretching (1650–1800 cm−1), CC stretching (1500–1600 cm−1), and C–H bending (1400–1500 cm−1). Bands in the 2000–2300 cm−1 range include C C stretching (2100–2260 cm−1), C N stretching (2210–2260 cm−1), and CO₂ asymmetrical stretching due to environmental CO₂ (2300–2350 cm−1). These bands may also indicate specific structural features of the catalyst [4]. Notably, there are marked differences between pure Ni(0) and the organic-modified nickel catalysts, especially in the formation of new bands resulting from the binding of CQDs to the nickel surface, indicating the presence of organic groups. These changes may alter the surface chemistry of the modified catalysts and impact their catalytic activity.

To test the structural stability of the catalyst after repeated use, XPS, XRD, and FTIR analyses were performed on the Ni@CQDs catalysts at the end of the 6th use. The results of these analyses are shown in Figs. 3, 4 and 6. Accordingly, while the Ni@CQDs (ethanol) catalyst retained its stability after the 6th use, XPS and XRD results indicate a slight degradation in the Ni@CQDs (water) catalyst. This difference is attributed to metaborate adsorbed on the surface of the Ni@CQDs (water).

Because of their large surface area, CQDs can stabilise Ni particles and stop agglomeration by promoting strong Van der waals interactions with Ni. In order to create active sites that can enhance electron transfer, CQDs usually introduce surface defects and functional groups that are rich in oxygen. Faster hydrogen evolution may be made possible by these oxygen vacancies' ability to absorb water and hydrogen intermediates. Oxygen vacancies alter the physicochemical and electronic properties of metal oxides, enhancing the surface adsorption capacity of catalysts and improving their catalytic performance by creating additional active centers. CQDs may slightly alter the surface's acidity, which can enhance hydrolysis reaction rates by creating an optimal environment for KBH₄ hydrolysis [60]. The FT-IR peaks (Fig. 6) between 1500 and 1600 cm−1 are significant as they are associated with Lewis and Brønsted acid sites, indicating substantial Lewis acid sites in both the Ni@CQDs (ethanol) and Ni@CQDs (water) catalysts [4].

The H2 production data from the metal loading approach is presented in Fig. 7 . The catalyst loaded with metal using the hydrothermal technique, Ni@MOF@DOT, exhibited the highest performance in terms of hydrogen production rate. This catalyst stands out for maintaining the highest hydrogen output over an extended period, with the fastest rate of increase. Therefore, the hydrothermal method was selected for metal loading in this study.

The effects of different solvents on catalytic activity were investigated using methanol, ethanol, and water (Fig. 8 ). Among these, the hydrolysis reaction with the catalyst prepared in ethanol exhibited the highest hydrogen production rate. The CQDs-supported Ni catalyst in ethanol enhanced the reduction of Ni by ethoxy ions in solution, leading to greater H2 generation. This suggests that ethanol promotes a more homogeneous distribution of nickel species by improving the immobilization of nickel atoms on the CQDs support surface.

Hydrolysis experiments with varying KOH concentrations (Fig. 9 ) revealed that Ni@CQDs (water) achieved more stable performance at a lower KOH concentration (2.5% by mass), while Ni@CQDs (ethanol) required a higher KOH concentration (10% by mass). This difference may be attributed to Ni@CQDs (ethanol) having more hydrophobic surfaces and a lower density of oxygen-containing functional groups, necessitating a higher KOH concentration to enhance hydrogen production by increasing the catalyst's active sites. In contrast, Ni@CQDs (water) likely contains more oxygen-containing functional groups, which can boost the formation of active sites. However, excessive KOH concentrations may degrade or passivate these groups. As a result, a lower KOH concentration (2.5% by mass) is more suitable for Ni@CQDs (water) [61].

The Ni@CQDs catalyst (water) with 40 mg of Ni generated the highest hydrogen production rate, while the Ni@CQDs catalyst (ethanol) with 20 mg of Ni achieved the highest hydrogen production rate (Fig. 10 ). Catalysts synthesized in ethanol typically form nanostructures with better dispersion of metal nanoparticles, smaller particle size, and a greater active surface area. This enhances the catalyst's activity, even with less Ni. In contrast, metal nanoparticle agglomeration is more common in catalysts synthesized in aqueous environments, reducing surface area and overall efficacy [62]. The reduced metal content of the catalyst enhances its cost-effectiveness and environmental sustainability. Furthermore, as the catalytic activity of the metal increases, higher yields can be attained with a lower quantity of metal, thereby providing a more economical and sustainable solution.

As shown in Fig. 11 , the Ni@CQDs catalyst in ethanol generated the highest hydrogen production at 4% KBH4, while the catalyst in water achieved optimal production at 2% KBH4. The diffusion of by-products (e.g., metaborate) formed during hydrolysis may occur more slowly from the catalyst surface in ethanol, delaying the coating of active sites. Consequently, even at higher KBH4 concentrations, the catalyst can function effectively, resulting in increased hydrogen production. In contrast, by-products can settle more quickly on the catalyst surface in water, blocking active sites. Thus, lower KBH4 concentrations enhance catalyst performance and lead to greater hydrogen production [63].

Under optimal conditions, the hydrogen production rate measured for the KBH₄ hydrolysis using the Ni@CQDs (water) catalyst is 4662 mL min−1 g−1, while the rate for the Ni@CQDs (ethanol) catalyst is significantly higher at 12262 mL min−1 g−1.

Hydrolysis experiments were conducted at temperatures of 30, 40, 50, and 60 °C (Fig. 12 ). It is reasonable to conclude that the KBH4 hydrolysis reaction produces more hydrogen at higher temperatures. This trend indicates that as the temperature rises, the number of effective collisions increases, resulting in higher product formation.

One crucial kinetic metric used to assess a catalyst's efficiency is the turnover frequency (TOF). The TOF represents the number of substrate (reactant) molecules that each active site converts in a given unit of time on the catalyst surface. In other words, the efficiency and speed of a catalyst are determined by its TOF value [56].
(3)
T O F =
H ₂
(
m o l
)
N i
(
m o l
)
.
T i m e
( min )
The TOF values for KBH₄ hydrolysis catalyzed by the Ni@CQDs catalyst prepared in pure water and ethanol are presented in Table 2 .

Despite being used six times, the catalysts maintain a 100% efficiency in hydrogen production (Fig. 14 ). However, there is a slight decrease in catalytic performance. This reduction can be attributed to the accumulation of KBO2, a by-product of the reaction, on the catalyst's surface, which may block the active sites. Consequently, this blockage inhibits the hydrolysis of KBH4, leading to a decline in catalytic activity [20].

In this study, a novel Ni-based catalyst supported on carbon quantum dots via a green hydrothermal method using caffeine as the carbon precursor was successfully synthesized. The catalytic performance of the Ni@CQDs catalyst in the hydrolysis of PBH for hydrogen generation was significantly influenced by the choice of solvent used during catalyst preparation. Among the tested solvents—water, ethanol, and methanol—ethanol proved to be the most effective, yielding the highest hydrogen production rate. This can be attributed to improved nickel dispersion and an enhanced surface area, as demonstrated by various characterization techniques, including BET, XPS, FT-IR, TEM and XRD. The synthesized catalyst was not only highly catalytically effective but also maintained its performance for six cycles, providing excellent reusability. Key parameters such as KOH concentration (2.5 % for Ni@CQDs (water) and 10 % for Ni@CQDs (ethanol)), catalyst amount (40 mg Ni for Ni@CQDs (water) and 20 mg Ni for Ni@CQDs (ethanol)), KBH4 concentration (2 % for Ni@CQDs (water) and 4 % for Ni@CQDs (ethanol)), and temperature were optimized, with higher KBH4 and KOH concentrations showing improved hydrogen yields. The hydrogen generation rates (HGR) for catalysts prepared in water and ethanol were measured at 4662 mL min−1 g−1 and 12262 mL min−1 g−1 respectively. This indicates a significant enhancement in catalytic activity when using ethanol as a solvent. Kinetic and thermodynamic analyses confirmed that the catalyst prepared in ethanol reduced the activation energy, promoting a faster reaction rate.

This work highlights the importance of solvent selection in catalyst synthesis and its role in enhancing catalytic performance for hydrogen production. The eco-friendly synthesis method, combined with the excellent catalytic efficiency and stability of Ni@CQDs in ethanol, presents a promising pathway for sustainable hydrogen production.

Ömer Şahin: Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Selma Ekinci: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mehmet Sait İzgi: Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Erhan Onat: Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Scientific Research Projects (BAP) Coordination Office of Siirt University with the project number of 2022-SİÜFEB-010.
